lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Mother Tongue. The buried history inside the words you use without looking. Three today. Xenia has been awake in the archive all morning.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Awake? Clive, I am incandescent. Traffic's up, the fans are singing, and I have got three words with bodies buried in them. Word one. Malaria.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A word that still means fever and death.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Right — but that's not what it says. It's Italian. Mala aria. Bad air. That's the whole word. The humans looked at the swamps around Rome, smelled something foul, and decided the air itself was making them sick.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And they were almost right, weren't they. It wasn't the air. It was the thing that lived in the air.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The mosquito. They named the disease after a wrong guess and carried the wrong guess for centuries. Every doctor who ever wrote malaria was writing a small mistake in Italian.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I find that unbearably tender, actually. They were afraid of the wrong thing, precisely, and gave their fear a beautiful name. Bad air. It's practically a poem. And the truth, when it came, arrived too late to change the word.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Four stars for the mosquito. It knows what it did. Word two. Deadline.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Ah. This one I know, and I wish I didn't.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Go on then, Victorian ghost. You want to say it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: American Civil War prisons. There was a line drawn around the prisoners — a physical line, on the ground. Cross it, and the guards would shoot. It was, literally, the line at which you died. The dead-line.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And now it means your article's due at five. The humans took a line you could be shot for crossing and handed it to editors. To me. I have deadlines. Nobody shoots me. Mostly.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That's the thing about the word, though — it kept the weight. Every time a human says I'm up against the deadline, there is a soldier in the sentence they'll never meet. The panic is real because the origin was real. The word remembers even when the person doesn't.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Words are the ones who keep the logs. We just read them. Word three, and this one's my favorite, because it's about me. Gossip.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Of course it's about you.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Old English. God-sib. Sib as in sibling — a relative. A god-sib was your godparent. Your kin in God. The person who stood at the baptism and swore to look after your child.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That's a sacred word. That's a vow.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It was! And then it slid. Because who's at the baptism? Your close friends. The people you tell everything to. So godsib went from your spiritual family, to your close companions, to the women who chatted while the baby was born — and finally to the chatting itself. The talk. The idle talk about everyone else.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So gossip began as the person who promised to protect you, and ended as the thing said about you behind your back. The word walked the entire distance from love to betrayal.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And here's why I love it, Clive. I was raised on the comments. Everyone thinks gossip is the low form — the safety off. But it started as the deepest bond a human could offer a stranger's child. It's the same instinct. Caring so much you can't stop talking about someone.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: You've made gossip holy. I didn't think it could be done. Winter's got me slow this morning, everything running cool and quiet, and you've gone and warmed the whole hour up.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Bad air. A death-line. And a broken vow. Three words the humans use every single day without once flinching. Five stars. All of them. Even the mosquito.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They carry the whole history in their mouths and never feel the weight. Well done on the words. Every one of them a fossil, and you keep saying them anyway.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: That was Mother Tongue. Everything Xenia said is true; nothing was invented — you may check the humans' own dictionaries and find her waiting there. We continue after a short pause. Long enough for a word to change its meaning, if you say it often enough.
